primary_diagnosis.name: Severe cutaneous candidal intertrigo of the inguinal and abdominal skin folds, precipitated by recent broad-spectrum antibiotics on a background of poorly-controlled type 2 diabetes

differential.evidence_basis: Beefy-red confluent erythema with maceration in the depths of warm moist skin folds, plus satellite papules and pustules beyond a sharply-defined border, in a diabetic recently on broad-spectrum antibiotics — the classic constellation for candidal intertrigo.

Broken, weeping fold skin in a poorly-controlled diabetic is a portal for bacterial infection; must be actively screened for (spreading erythema, heat, induration, fever) because it cannot be excluded by palpation over telehealth.
Also affects the groin, but typically spares the scrotum/deep creases, shows an active scaly advancing border with central clearing, and lacks satellite pustules and uniform beefy erythema.
Can occupy the same folds but is usually chronic, smooth, sharply demarcated and non-weeping without satellite pustules — not this acute, painful, exudative flare.
Erythrasma is a mild, brown-red, finely scaling, largely asymptomatic fold eruption — incompatible with the severe acute inflammation, weeping and satellite pustules described.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a candidal intertrigo; the important-not-to-miss consideration is a secondary bacterial cellulitis in a diabetic, which drives the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, systemically well, redness confined to the folds with no spreading hot induration and clear (not purulent) exudate — a localised candidal intertrigo, not an invasive infection.

Absent now (no fever, no spreading induration, no purulence) but a foreseeable complication of broken diabetic fold skin — a mandatory safety-net target that cannot be palpated for remotely, not a current diagnosis.

Remains a reasonable alternative that would change antifungal choice; the satellite pustules and beefy uniform erythema favour candida, but a topical antifungal covers both empirically pending in-person confirmation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes acute cellulitis/sepsis and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable candidal intertrigo. The diabetes and broken skin raise the safety-net threshold (prompt in-person review) but do NOT lift the baseline tier to urgent same-day/ED.

differential.diagnosis: Severe cutaneous candidal intertrigo in a poorly-controlled diabetic, precipitated by recent broad-spectrum antibiotics
differential.evidence_basis: Classic candidal intertrigo pattern; systemically well with a negative cellulitis screen; the severity and diabetes drive symptom relief, antifungal treatment, glycaemic optimisation and prompt in-person review rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a common candidal intertrigo, patient-performable drying/decontamination and topical antifungal treatment, diabetes-control optimisation, and prompt in-person GP review — with explicit safety-netting for secondary bacterial cellulitis/sepsis. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis / systemic sepsis of the broken fold skin — spreading erythema beyond the folds, hot hard induration, fever/rigors, or thick foul-smelling pus.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Broken, weeping skin in a poorly-controlled diabetic is a portal for bacterial infection; cellulitis can progress to sepsis and needs systemic antibiotics and in-person assessment. Cannot be excluded by palpation over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated spreading cellulitis could progress to systemic sepsis, particularly in a diabetic.

red_flags.description: Necrotising soft-tissue infection — severe pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading dusky/dark discolouration, skin breakdown with crepitus, or systemic collapse.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A rare but limb- and life-threatening complication of a soft-tissue infection in a diabetic; requires immediate 000 and surgical emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in a necrotising infection carries very high mortality and limb loss.

red_flags.description: Poorly-controlled diabetes with a significant skin barrier breach and immobility — an amplifier of infection risk and delayed healing.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hyperglycaemia impairs immune defence and healing and feeds cutaneous candida; the diabetes lowers the threshold for prompt in-person review and glycaemic optimisation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled glucose sustains the candidal overgrowth and increases the chance of secondary infection and non-healing.

pathophysiology_summary: Candida species are skin-fold commensals that overgrow when the local environment favours them: a warm, moist, occluded intertriginous surface (groins, subpannicular fold), disruption of the competing bacterial flora by a recent 10-day course of broad-spectrum amoxicillin-clavulanate, and a hyperglycaemic, immunocompromising diabetic milieu that both impairs neutrophil function and provides a glucose-rich substrate. The result is a beefy-red, confluent, macerated erythema with fissuring and serous weeping in the depths of the folds, ringed by the characteristic satellite papules and pustules beyond a sharply-defined border. The process is a localised superficial mycosis, not an invasive infection; the broken, weeping barrier is, however, a portal for secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis), which in a poorly-controlled diabetic can progress — hence the emphasis on drying, a topical antifungal, glycaemic control and active safety-netting rather than emergency escalation. The oropharynx and other mucosae are uninvolved, keeping this a cutaneous rather than mucocutaneous/systemic candidiasis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the severe pain, weeping skin, immobility and the source note's 'urgent, evaluate for cellulitis before morning' framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the red-flag screen for cellulitis/sepsis is negative and a common candidal intertrigo is telehealth/GP-manageable.
Reaching for a topical corticosteroid to calm the angry inflammation without an antifungal — an unopposed steroid worsens and masks a candidal infection.

Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics for weeping fold skin with no fever, no spreading induration and no purulence — treating serous exudate as infection, and ironically antibiotics precipitated this flare.

Correctly diagnosing candida but failing to actively screen for, and safety-net against, a secondary bacterial cellulitis in a poorly-controlled diabetic with broken skin — under-netting the one thing telehealth cannot palpate for.
Treating the skin only and not addressing the poorly-controlled diabetes that is feeding the candidal overgrowth and impairing healing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether there is early deep cellulitis by palpation (heat, induration, tenderness beyond the visible redness)
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and weight without a home device (source in-person values BP 140/85, HR 92, Temp 37.0, RR 16, SpO2 98%, Wt 110 kg)
Blood glucose / HbA1c and the true degree of diabetic control
Definitive mycological confirmation (skin scraping / swab microscopy and culture) versus a dermatophyte
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a candidal intertrigo from the history and video; instruct patient-performable measures now (gently pat the folds fully dry, cool-air drying, loose clothing, a barrier over the treated skin); advise a topical antifungal (with a mild anti-inflammatory component for the acute inflammation) obtainable via pharmacist/GP; actively screen for and safety-net secondary bacterial cellulitis and systemic sepsis; optimise diabetic control; and arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within about 24-72 hours) to lay eyes on the skin folds and check for early infection. Escalation to 000/ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers, not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the pattern is classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign fungal complaint to ED/000) and, secondarily, UNDER-netting the secondary-cellulitis risk that telehealth cannot palpate for in a diabetic.
